readme.content:
# 😊 Happiness Tracker **A thinking tool for understanding what matters.** Define the areas of life that affect your happiness, assign importance,
score yourself honestly, and watch the math reveal what to focus on. [![Next.js](https://img.shields.io/badge/Next.js-15-black?logo=next.js)](https://nextjs.org/) [![React](https://img.shields.io/badge/React-19-61DAFB?logo=react)](https://react.dev/) [![TypeScript](https://img.shields.io/badge/TypeScript-5.9-3178C6?logo=typescript)](https://typescriptlang.org/) [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](LICENSE)
--- ## Why? Most happiness advice is generic. This tool takes the opposite approach — it asks *you* to define what matters and how much, then uses weighted scoring to surface where your life is strong and where it needs attention. The default categories are inspired by Maslow's hierarchy, but you can restructure everything to match your own mental model. ## How It Works The app follows a five-step workflow: | Step | Tab | What you do | |------|-----|-------------| | **1** | **Structure** | Define your life areas as a category tree (pillars → subcategories) | | **2** | **Prioritize** | Assign importance weights (1–10) to each area | | **3** | **Assess** | Score yourself 1–10 in every leaf category | | **4** | **Results** | See your weighted overall score, pillar breakdown, strengths, and attention areas | | **5** | **History** | Compare assessments over time and track trends | Scores bubble up through the tree using normalized sibling weights, so a low score in a high-priority area gets flagged automatically. ## Quick Start ```bash # Install dependencies npm install # Start the dev server npm run dev ``` Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) in your browser. ## Scripts | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `npm run dev` | Start development server with hot reload | | `npm run build` | Build for production (static export) | | `npm start` | Serve the production build | | `npm run lint` | Run Next.js linting | ## Project Structure ``` src/ ├── app/ │ ├── layout.tsx # Root layout with metadata │ ├── page.tsx # Main page (client-side SPA) │ └── globals.css # All styles + CSS custom properties ├── components/ │ ├── AssessmentFlow.tsx # Score input UI for each leaf category │ ├── CategoryTree.tsx # Editable tree of life areas │ ├── Dashboard.tsx # Results: overall score, pillars, insights │ ├── History.tsx # Past assessments timeline │ └── WeightEditor.tsx # Importance sliders per category ├── context/ │ └── AppContext.tsx # Global state via useReducer + auto-save ├── data/ │ └── starterContent.ts # Default Maslow-based category tree ├── models/ │ └── types.ts # TypeScript interfaces for all data └── services/ ├── calculator.ts # Weighted score computation engine └── storage.ts # localStorage persistence + JSON import/export ``` ## Key Design Decisions - **Client-only** — All data lives in `localStorage`. No server, no accounts, no tracking. Your data stays on your device. - **Static export** — Configured with `output: 'export'` in Next.js, so it can be deployed to any static host (GitHub Pages, Netlify, Vercel, etc.). - **Hierarchical weighted scoring** — Weights are normalized among siblings at each level, then multiplied up the tree. This means rearranging your categories changes how scores aggregate. - **Dark mode** — Automatic via `prefers-color-scheme` with CSS custom properties. ## Data Portability Use the **Export** / **Import** buttons in the header to back up or transfer your data as JSON. ## License MIT

readme.content:
# Runtime Storyboard Debugger

> **Status:** pre-1.0, public roadmap. See [docs/release-plan.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger/blob/main/docs/release-plan.md) and [docs/v1-scope.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger/blob/main/docs/v1-scope.md) for what's shipping and what's deferred.

Open an unfamiliar codebase in an editor that already knows where the entry points are, where the unfinished work is, and how the code actually behaves at runtime.

RSD is shaped like a code editor on purpose. Drop in a local folder or a public GitHub URL and you get:

- a familiar **Activity Bar / Sidebar / Editor / Inspector / Bottom Panel** layout
- a **file tree** that shows TODO/FIXME counts and which files contain entry points
- the project **README** opened in the editor on first load
- detected **entry points** with auto-generated input forms — one click runs them
- a **storyboard** of the run that decorates the source: gutter glyphs, active line highlights, frame-by-frame variables and side effects in the Inspector
- ⌘K **command palette** for jumping to any file, entry point, or finding

Runtime tracing is one tab, not the whole app. RSD is fully usable as a static explorer; the runtime storyboard is what you reach for when you want to know what actually happened, not just what could.

## Why This Exists

Most code-understanding tools force a tradeoff:

- static graphs show what could happen, but not what actually happened
- debuggers show what happened, but not in a repo-first, onboarding-friendly way
- LLM explanations can be helpful, but they often hide uncertainty or skip the underlying evidence

RSD makes the evidence visible first and the interpretation optional.

## What It Does

- Open a local path or a public GitHub repository URL.
- Browse files in a Monaco-based editor with TODO/FIXME and entry-point decorations rendered inline.
- Search the workspace with case-insensitive grep across allowlisted extensions.
- List unfinished-work findings (TODO/FIXME/HACK/stub/analysis-gap) grouped by kind.
- Run any detected entry point and watch frames stream into the Storyboard timeline.
- Click a runtime frame (in the editor gutter or the timeline) to see its captured variables and side effects in the Inspector.
- Fall back to deterministic static analysis when runtime tracing stalls or fails.
- Offer optional LLM assistance — additive, labeled, never replacing the deterministic baseline.

## How It Works

1. Create a workspace from a local directory or GitHub URL.
2. RSD ingests the repo, runs static analysis, and surfaces phase progress in the title bar.
3. The file tree, README, entry points and findings populate the editor as soon as the workspace is ready.
4. You pick an entry point, fill the auto-generated input form in the Inspector, and hit **▶ Run**.
5. RSD streams execution events into the storyboard tab and decorates the open source files as frames arrive.
6. If runtime tracing cannot complete, RSD shows the blocker and the best available fallback analysis without taking over the editor.

## Quick Start

Requires **Node.js 20+** and npm 10+.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ZSturman/Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger.git
cd Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger
npm install
npm run dev
```

`npm run dev` starts the API server (with `examples/order-api` preloaded) and the Vite UI together. Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000).

### Advanced: run the pieces separately

If you need to target a different repo or port, run the two halves directly.

Terminal 1 — API server:

```bash
npx ts-node packages/core/src/cli/index.ts analyze <path-to-target> --port 3001
```

Terminal 2 — UI:

```bash
cd packages/ui
RSD_API_URL=http://localhost:3001 npx vite
```

> **Path format**: the `analyze` argument must be an **absolute path** (e.g., `/Users/you/my-project`).
> Relative paths are resolved from the directory where npm was originally invoked, not from `packages/core`.

## Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Local path not found: …` on startup | The path passed to `analyze` doesn't exist or is relative and resolved from the wrong directory | Use an absolute path: `analyze /abs/path/to/project` |
| `git: command not found` during GitHub ingestion | `git` is not on your PATH | Install git and re-run |
| GitHub workspace fails with permission error | Private repository — not supported yet | Clone the repo locally and use a local-path workspace |
| `No entry points detected` after analysis | Target is not a Node.js/Express app, or source files are in an unexpected location | Ensure the project has a `src/` folder with exported functions or Express route handlers |
| Port already in use | Another process is listening on port 3001 | Pass `--port <other>` to the `analyze` command |
| UI connects but shows a stale workspace | A previous `npm run dev` left the server running | Kill the old process and restart |

## Recommended Demo Targets

- Local: `examples/order-api`
- GitHub: `mjgs/minimal-express-typescript`
- GitHub: `expressjs/express`
- GitHub: `gothinkster/node-express-realworld-example-app`

The last two are especially useful for testing graceful fallback when runtime setup is incomplete or expensive.

## UI Flow

### Workspace Intake

- Choose `Local path` or `GitHub URL`
- Create a workspace
- Watch ingestion and analysis progress instead of waiting on a blank state

### Workspace Overview

- Review likely journeys
- Inspect detected entry points
- Check runtime blockers and detected scripts
- Optionally configure LLM assistance

### Live Execution

- Start an explicit trace for a supported route or exported function
- Watch the timeline fill with status updates, calls, branches, waits, logs, snapshots, side effects, returns, and failures
- Scrub through captured steps while the run is still active
- Click any branch you didn't take to re-run with inputs aimed at it

### Keyboard Shortcuts

| Key | Action |
| --- | --- |
| `←` / `→` | Previous / next step |
| `↑` / `↓` | Jump back / forward 5 steps |
| `F` | Focus the flow map |
| `/` | Filter the timeline |
| `C` | Re-run with different inputs |
| `B` | Back to workspace overview |
| `?` | Open the keyboard shortcut sheet |
| `Esc` | Close overlays / clear filter |

### Fallback Analysis

- If execution fails or stalls, RSD shows the blocker clearly
- The flow graph, unfinished work, and other static artifacts stay visible so the workspace still feels trustworthy

## Optional LLM Assistance

> **Note for v1.0:** the LLM panel is being removed from the v1.0 launch and reintroduced post-launch. The code still exists in the current build but is not part of the supported flow. See [docs/release-plan.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger/blob/main/docs/release-plan.md).

LLM help is opt-in and session-only.

- Providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, OpenRouter
- API keys are kept in memory only
- Model lists are fetched when the provider supports it, otherwise curated defaults are shown
- The LLM layer is used for explanation and prioritization, not for baseline repo ingestion or core tracing

## Architecture

```text
packages/
  core/
    analyzer/        entry points, unfinished work, flow graphs
    instrumenter/    runtime tracing + Babel instrumentation
    server/          workspace sessions, GitHub ingestion, SSE streams, execution sessions, LLM endpoints
    storyboard/      frame building + narration
  ui/
    src/components/  intake, loading, overview, live execution, step inspection
examples/
  order-api/         sample local app for end-to-end exploration
tests/
  unit/              analyzer, instrumenter, storyboard, server helpers
```

## Screenshots And Diagrams

For a stronger GitHub presentation, add these assets near the top of the README:

- A hero screenshot of the live execution workspace while a trace is actively streaming
- A screenshot of the workspace overview showing journeys, entry points, blockers, and scripts
- A short GIF of a run progressing from analysis to live execution to fallback
- A simple architecture diagram showing `Ingest -> Analyze -> Trace -> Explain`

If you add screenshots, place them directly under `What It Does` and keep captions short and outcome-oriented.

## Current Scope

Included now:

- local path ingestion
- public GitHub repo ingestion
- automatic entry-point and journey discovery
- live execution streaming with inspectable steps
- workspace-level fallback states
- optional multi-provider LLM assist flow

Not included yet:

- private GitHub repo auth
- automatic remote runtime execution without explicit user action
- persistent LLM credential storage
- multi-language analysis beyond JS/TS
- IDE integration
- collaborative annotations and sharing

## Validation

```bash
npm run lint
npm test
npm run build
```

## License

[MIT](https://github.com/ZSturman/Runtime-Storyboard-Debugger/blob/main/LICENSE) © Zachary Sturman

## Design Principles

- Fast repo understanding beats hidden magic
- Live progress beats vague loading states
- Trustworthy fallback beats broken execution
- Static evidence comes before LLM interpretation
- Technical detail stays available without overwhelming non-developers

# Folio Archive

![Folio app icon](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/main/Reference/AppIcon/AppIcon.png)

This repository is an archive of related but independent attempts to solve the same personal problem: how to keep projects, ideas, media, metadata, references, and portfolio material in one system without losing the shape of the work.

The important thing to know is that this is not a neat release train. It is closer to a shelf of short stories with a family resemblance. Some branches are polished enough to run. Some are prototypes that taught a single lesson. Some are abandoned sketches that still explain why the later work looks the way it does.

Dates are approximate when they come from filesystem timestamps instead of Git history. The full narrative source is in [docs/HISTORY.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/docs/HISTORY.md).

The current visual identity for the project lives in [Reference/AppIcon/AppIcon.png](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Reference/AppIcon/AppIcon.png), which is included here so the repo front page has a clear visual anchor instead of reading like a raw filesystem dump.

## Start Here

| If you want to... | Go here |
| --- | --- |
| Understand the overall arc | [docs/HISTORY.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/docs/HISTORY.md) |
| See the organization system of the repo | [Current/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Current/README.md), [Archive/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/README.md), [Experiments/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Experiments/README.md) |
| Open the most complete app | [Current/FolioStudio/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Current/FolioStudio/README.md) |
| Explore the historical branches | [Archive/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/README.md) |
| Browse supporting assets and legacy workspace material | [Reference/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Reference/README.md) |

## Organization System

Every major branch in this repository follows the same documentation pattern.

- `Dates`: the span of time the branch seems to belong to.
- `Status`: active, archived, prototype, abandoned, spike, or reference-only.
- `Question`: the problem that branch was trying to answer.
- `What survives here`: the code, notes, or assets that still matter.
- `What it led to`: the next branch, if one exists.

At the repo level, the structure is intentionally simple.

- [Current](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Current/README.md): the most complete product path.
- [Archive](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/README.md): historical versions, concept families, and abandoned attempts.
- [Experiments](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Experiments/README.md): side branches and spikes that mattered enough to keep.
- [Reference](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Reference/README.md): app icons, setup captures, and legacy workspace material.
- [docs](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/docs/README.md): long-form narrative and archive notes.

## Map Of The Archive

| Storyline | Dates | Status | What it is | Read more |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Current Folio Studio | Nov 2025 to Mar 2026 | Active snapshot | The clearest, most complete macOS document-based portfolio editor in the archive | [Current/FolioStudio/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Current/FolioStudio/README.md) |
| IdeasApp | Sep 2025 to Oct 2025 | Archived prototype | Idea capture, triage, and project scaffolding separated from long-term project management | [Archive/IdeasApp/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/IdeasApp/README.md) |
| OPE Metadata Systems | Sep 2022 to Jan 2026 | Archived concept family | The broadest thinking branch: wireframes, notes, filesystem-first organizers, and schema experiments | [Archive/OPE-Metadata-Systems/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/OPE-Metadata-Systems/README.md) |
| Obsidian Project Management App | Jul 2024 to Sep 2024 | Abandoned prototype | A Tauri desktop attempt to bridge local projects, ideas, and markdown tools like Obsidian | [Archive/Obsidian-Project-Management-App/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/Obsidian-Project-Management-App/README.md) |
| PortfolioEditor | Oct 2025 to Dec 2025 | Archived prototype | A simpler document-based JSON editor that narrowed the scope before current Folio | [Archive/PortfolioEditor/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/PortfolioEditor/README.md) |
| RPOVault | Oct 2025 to Dec 2025 | Archived spike | A very small SwiftUI and SwiftData experiment kept as context rather than as a product | [Experiments/RPOVault/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Experiments/RPOVault/README.md) |

## How To Read This Repo

If you want the shortest path, start with [Current/FolioStudio/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Current/FolioStudio/README.md) and then jump backward into [Archive/PortfolioEditor/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/PortfolioEditor/README.md) and [Archive/OPE-Metadata-Systems/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/Archive/OPE-Metadata-Systems/README.md).

If you want the full story, read [docs/HISTORY.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Folio-Studio/blob/main/docs/HISTORY.md) and then use the README files in each branch as chapter cards for the surviving folders.

If you are here for technical context more than narrative, the most useful branches are current Folio, PortfolioEditor, Portfolio Organizer, and the browser editor in the OPE family.

title: Mass and Time
name: Mass and Time
summary:
I’m treating this as a serious personal inquiry rather than a finished theory. The project turns a bold idea, that time may help generate or stabilize what we call mass, into three concrete modeling tracks: a feedback simulation, a minimal quantum stability model, and a relativistic conceptual visualization. The useful part is that the idea has to face equations, parameter choices, and saved outputs instead of staying at the level of language.

The audience is people who are comfortable with scientific reasoning and code, including technically curious readers, independent researchers, and physics-adjacent builders who value inspectable assumptions over polished certainty. What makes it distinct is the tone and structure: I keep the claim ambitious, then narrow it carefully, and I separate what is computed from what is interpretive. Some uncertainty is still central, especially around physical realism, conservation behavior, and how far these toy models can responsibly be taken.
oneLiner: A simulation-backed physics project that tests whether temporal persistence can help explain mass, orbital stability, and gravity, while staying honest about what is model output versus interpretation.

assets.label: Horizon Zoom: Extreme Gravitational Time Ratio
assets.summary: This horizon zoom focuses on the region near the black hole’s Schwarzschild radius, where gravitational time effects become more extreme. Tightening the radial window around the horizon reduced the minimum gravitational time ratio from 0.0995 to 0.0316, emphasizing how rapidly temporal behavior changes near this boundary. This image works well as a technical companion to the broader photon–black hole duality map. It gives viewers a more focused look at the extreme end of the model, where spatial confinement, horizon behavior, and temporal distortion become most visually pronounced.
assets.oneLiner: A close-up of the black-hole horizon region showing intensified gravitational time distortion.

assets.label: Photon–Black Hole Duality Map
assets.summary: This image presents the photon–black hole duality simulation, which compares horizon behavior, lightlike motion, and a broader polarity diagram in a single figure. The default configuration models a 10-solar-mass black hole, corresponding to a Schwarzschild radius of 29.538 km. Conceptually, the visualization frames photons and black holes as opposite endpoints: photon-like behavior as an extreme associated with lightlike motion, and black-hole-like behavior as an extreme associated with horizon-level spatial confinement. This is one of the most portfolio-friendly visuals because it translates a complex theoretical idea into a clear geometric spectrum.
assets.oneLiner: A polarity diagram comparing lightlike motion and black-hole horizon behavior as opposite geometric limits.

assets.label: Orbital Temporal Stability and Resonance
assets.summary: This image visualizes the orbital temporal stability experiment, which tests how a simple orbital system responds to temporal perturbation. Because QuTiP was not installed in the simulation environment, the run used a manual NumPy RK4 solver. The default perturbed run produced variance of 0.1231 with a maximum deviation of 0.6535, compared with a cleaner control run that reached a lower maximum deviation of 0.5115. The simulation sweep peaked near ω = 0.1000, suggesting a resonance region worth further investigation. In the portfolio, this image helps show that the project is not only conceptual but also tests the stability implications of temporal variation.
assets.oneLiner: A minimal orbital model testing whether temporal perturbations produce instability or resonance bands.

assets.label: High-Alpha Clock Distortion Test
assets.summary: This image shows the final state of the mass-time emergence model under a stronger alpha setting. Increasing alpha to 0.5 deepened the slowest local clock rate from 0.7955 in the baseline run to 0.0001, creating a much more extreme temporal distortion. This comparison is useful because it shows how sensitive the model is to temporal coupling strength. In the portfolio narrative, this image can serve as a “parameter stress test,” demonstrating that the simulation is not just a static visualization but a tunable model whose behavior changes substantially under different assumptions.
assets.oneLiner: A stress test showing how stronger temporal coupling dramatically slows the local clock field.

assets.label: Animated Mass-Time Emergence
assets.summary: This animation shows the time evolution of the mass-time emergence model. Instead of presenting a single final state, it reveals how the field changes step by step as mass presence interacts with a locally varying clock structure. The animation is useful as the main visual anchor for the project because it gives viewers an intuitive sense of emergence, feedback, and field evolution. It communicates the idea that mass is not merely placed into a fixed background, but may be modeled as something that changes alongside local temporal conditions.
assets.oneLiner: An animation of mass presence evolving dynamically in response to local changes in clock rate.

assets.label: Mass-Time Emergence: Baseline Field Evolution
assets.summary: This image shows the final state of a baseline mass-time simulation. The model evolves an initial mass distribution against a clock field whose local rate changes over time, allowing the simulation to test whether slowed local time can reshape mass presence and generate a gravitational response. In this run, total mass increased from 1.0000 to 1.6140 while producing a maximum gravitational potential magnitude of 0.1836. As a portfolio visual, this image represents the project’s core hypothesis: that mass and gravitational behavior can be explored as emergent effects of temporal structure rather than treated only as static spatial quantities.
assets.oneLiner: A baseline simulation showing how mass presence evolves within a locally distorted clock field.

assets.label: Simulation Overview: Time, Mass, and Geometric Extremes
assets.summary: This overview contact sheet presents the full simulation batch as a unified visual system. It brings together three experimental models that explore different aspects of the Time and Mass framework: how mass presence might evolve under locally varying clock rates, how temporal perturbations may affect orbital stability, and how photons and black holes can be interpreted as opposite geometric limits. The image functions as a high-level map of the project, giving viewers an immediate sense of the visual language, simulation outputs, and conceptual scope before they enter the individual experiments.
assets.oneLiner: A visual summary of the project’s three simulation tracks: mass-time emergence, temporal orbital stability, and photon–black hole duality.

# Time and Mass: A Time-Centric Ontology of Mass, Gravity, and Spacetime

> *Space is where mass is. Time is why mass is. Gravity is how mass is felt.*

## Overview

This project explores a speculative but structured physics hypothesis: **time is the fundamental substrate from which mass emerges**. Mass is not a static property but a persistent resonance of quantum-field excitations sustained through continuous temporal evolution. Gravity, in turn, is the wave-like propagation of that persistence through spacetime.

The hypothesis inverts the standard GR causal arrow: instead of "mass warps time," it proposes "time generates mass."

## Core Ideas (7 Pillars)

| # | Pillar | Key Claim |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | [Time Generates Mass](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/01_time_generates_mass.md) | Mass emerges from persistent quantum excitations evolving through time |
| 2 | [Mass Flux Density](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/02_mass_flux_density.md) | (kg/s)/m captures gravitational character better than static mass |
| 3 | [Relativistic Mass Distribution](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/03_relativistic_mass_distribution.md) | At relativistic speeds, mass becomes spatially non-localized |
| 4 | [Gravity = Wave, Mass = Particle](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/04_gravity_wave_mass_particle.md) | Wave-particle duality extends to gravity-mass relationship |
| 5 | [Temporal Resonance Orbitals](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/05_temporal_resonance_orbitals.md) | Electron orbitals are stable because temporal interference averages to zero |
| 6 | [Photon–Black Hole Duality](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/06_photon_blackhole_duality.md) | Photons (pure time) and black holes (pure space) are extremes of a polarity spectrum |
| 7 | [Strong Force as Temporal Gradient](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/07_strong_force_temporal_gradient.md) | Nuclear binding from short-range temporal coherence equilibration |

Full synthesis: [theory/00_core_hypothesis.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/00_core_hypothesis.md)

## Simulations

Three numerical simulations test aspects of this hypothesis. Each has adjustable parameters via YAML config files, a Python CLI script, and a Jupyter notebook.

### Quick Start

```bash
cd simulations
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Run any simulation:
python mass_time_emergence/simulation.py
python orbital_temporal_stability/simulation.py
python photon_blackhole_duality/visualization.py

# Or generate a full headless batch with saved artifacts:
python run_batch.py

# Or use Jupyter notebooks for interactive exploration:
jupyter notebook
```

### Available Simulations

| Simulation | Tests Pillars | Description |
|---|---|---|
| [Mass-Time Emergence](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/mass_time_emergence/) | 1, 2 | 1D model of mass density evolving from time flow with gravitational coupling |
| [Orbital Temporal Stability](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/orbital_temporal_stability/) | 5 | QuTiP two-level atom: does temporal perturbation destabilize orbitals? |
| [Photon–BH Duality](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/photon_blackhole_duality/) | 6 | Schwarzschild metric visualization showing space-time polarity spectrum |

All parameters are adjustable in each simulation's `config.yaml`.

## Directory Structure

```
Time and Mass/
├── README.md                    ← You are here
├── PLAN.md                      ← Implementation plan (phases, status)
├── theory/                      ← 8 curated theory documents
├── simulations/
│   ├── requirements.txt
│   ├── shared/config_loader.py  ← Shared YAML config utility
│   ├── mass_time_emergence/     ← Simulation 1
│   ├── orbital_temporal_stability/  ← Simulation 2
│   └── photon_blackhole_duality/    ← Simulation 3
├── analysis/                    ← Future: real data pipelines
├── notes/
│   ├── journal/                 ← Dated entries (2025_07_01, etc.)
│   └── thought_experiments/     ← Idea sketches (rain analogy, gravity, etc.)
├── references/
│   ├── chatgpt_discussions/     ← AI-assisted exploration transcripts
│   ├── literature_to_review.md  ← Books, papers, datasets to study
│   ├── proposed_experiments.md  ← Testable experiments (at-home + funded)
│   └── Mass in a timeless universe.pdf
├── images/                      ← Diagrams and screenshots
└── archive/                     ← Original files (preserved verbatim)
```

## Future Work (Phase 4+)

- Relativistic mass delocalization simulation
- 2D gravity wave propagation
- CERN Open Data analysis (muon momentum distributions)
- Atomic clock / GPS data correlation
- LIGO gravitational wave analysis
- Machine learning pattern detection

See [PLAN.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/PLAN.md) for the full roadmap.

summary: ChewSense is an iOS app that helps people slow down while they eat and pay more attention to their habits. It uses the motion sensors in AirPods to pick up chewing—mainly through X-axis acceleration—and gives gentle alerts when someone might be eating too fast. The goal is simple: improve digestion, avoid overeating, and make meals feel more mindful without asking the user to track anything manually.

readme.content:
# Chew Sense – Data Collection and Labeling

Chew Sense records synchronized AirPods motion data and video for research, prototyping, and dataset creation. While optimized for chewing-related motion analysis, it can support any workflow requiring aligned video and AirPods motion samples.

---

## Features

### Automatic AirPods Connection
- The app connects to supported AirPods motion sensors on launch.  
- Recording is enabled only when motion data is actively available.

### Synchronized Recording
- Each session stores a `.mov` video file and a `.csv` motion-data file in a single folder.  
- Timestamps ensure precise alignment between modalities.

### Optional Labeling Workflow
- Recordings can be exported immediately when started in **Not eating** mode.  
- When labeling is required, the detail view supports:
  - Playback  
  - Marker placement  
  - Segment-based boolean labels  
- Segments between markers are labeled `true`; all others are `false`.

### Connection-Loss Protection
- The app automatically pauses recording if the AirPods connection drops.  
- This prevents incomplete or misaligned datasets.

---

## Data Format

- **Video:** `recording.mov`  
- **Motion data:** `motion.csv` with timestamped sensor rows  
- **Labels:** Boolean values aligned to motion-data segments  

---

## Exporting

Recordings can be exported or shared as a folder containing both files. This design simplifies downstream processing and machine-learning dataset assembly.

---

## Intended Use

Chew Sense is designed for technical users who need clean, consistent, and synchronized motion/video data.  
It is **not** intended as a consumer chewing-monitoring or health-tracking product.

readme.content:
**TowardBetter Announcement — Static Demonstration Site**

This repository contains a static announcement/landing page built to demonstrate design and frontend engineering skills. It's not a full product backend — it's a polished, component-driven static site showing what I can deliver: layout, responsive UI, animations, forms, and small serverless routes for demo purposes.

**What This Is**
- **Purpose:** A static marketing/announcement page intended as a demonstration of frontend craft and product thinking.
- **Stack:** Next.js (App Router), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, modular React components, and a small set of serverless API routes for demo form/email flows.
- **Static-first:** The site is built to be exported/deployed as a static site or hosted on serverless platforms (Vercel, Netlify). There is no production database — all dynamic examples are self-contained and demo-focused.

**Highlights / Features**
- **Design & Layout:** Clean, responsive landing layout with a hero, features, FAQ, and CTA sections.
- **Component Library:** Reusable components in the `components/` folder (cards, forms, buttons, dialogs, inputs).
- **Interactions & Accessibility:** Keyboard accessible UI, lightbox, parallax image, and subtle animations for an engaging experience.
- **Forms & Demo API:** Email/Signup forms wired to demo serverless routes under `app/api/` to show end-to-end flow without a full backend.
- **Content Modules:** Central content and copy live in the `site/` folder for easy editing and reuse.

**Project Structure (short)**
- `app/` — Next.js app entry, global layout, and top-level routes.
- `components/` — UI components organized by purpose (containers, forms, ui primitives).
- `site/` — Content modules (features, faqs, media, pitch deck data).
- `styles/` — Tailwind config and global styles.
- `public/` — Static images and assets.

**Run locally**
1. Install dependencies:

	`npm install`

2. Run development server:

	`npm run dev`

3. Build for production:

	`npm run build`

4. Start the production server (if needed):

	`npm run start`

Notes: This project uses Next.js and is intended to be previewed locally or deployed to a static/serverless host. If you want a fully static export, you can use hosting options that support Next.js static output or run an export process depending on your preferred hosting provider.

**Editing & Extending**
- Update copy and structured content in the `site/` folder.
- Add or modify components in `components/` and styles in `styles/`.
- Serverless demo endpoints live in `app/api/` — they are lightweight examples and can be replaced with real APIs if you connect a backend.

**Deployment**
- Recommended: Deploy to Vercel for best compatibility with Next.js app and serverless functions.
- Alternatives: Netlify or other static hosts (may require build/export configuration).

**Why this repo**
This project exists to show a complete, polished static announcement page that you can use as a starting point or a live portfolio sample. It demonstrates:
- Frontend engineering with modern React/Next.js patterns
- Component-driven UI and design consistency
- Attention to accessibility, responsive design, and micro-interactions
- Practical demo of connecting forms to serverless endpoints

**Contact / Attribution**
If you'd like to discuss this project, request changes, or see more work, reach out to the author directly (contact details in my portfolio). This repository is a demonstration and may be updated periodically to reflect new experiments and UI ideas.

---

title: Lost & Hound
name: Lost & Hound
subtitle: Find your Furry Friend
summary: Lost & Hound is a mobile application designed to make it easier to reconnect lost pets with the people looking for them. Instead of relying on flyers, chance encounters, or social media posts that quickly disappear, the app provides a centralized place to report lost pets and log sightings in real time. Users can post details, photos, and locations, browse nearby reports, and reach out directly when something looks like a match.The app currently only works in Australia, reflecting where the initial rollout and testing took place. The project was built as a full-stack React Native application with a Node backend and Firebase for data and authentication. One team extended the system further by integrating computer vision, allowing uploaded images of spotted animals to be compared against photos of missing pets. The goal was not to replace human judgment, but to reduce friction and shorten the path between seeing an animal and contacting the right person.
oneLiner: A community-driven mobile app that helps reunite lost pets with their owners by turning scattered, offline sightings into a shared, searchable system. The live app currently operates in Australia.

title: Obsidian Toolkit
name: Obsidian Toolkit
summary: This project is a modular toolkit designed to make working inside Obsidian more dynamic and visual. It includes a dashboard system modeled after how Next.js handles routing, turning your folder structure into a navigable home view that mirrors the way web frameworks organize content. Alongside it is QueueCards, a note queueing system that helps prioritize ideas, tasks, or references by tracking how often items are skipped. Together, they create a workspace that’s both structured and adaptable, aimed at people who like to think in systems and want their vault to feel more like a living interface than a static archive.
oneLiner: A modular Obsidian toolkit that turns your vault into an interactive dashboard and adds a simple queue system for managing notes. It’s an experiment in blending web-style structure with personal note-taking.

# The-2nd-Best-Obsidian-Collection

This repo is a growing collection of Obsidian resources designed to enhance how you interact with your notes and manage your information. 

## Current Features

### Dashboard

The `Dashboard` feature is a dynamic way to visualize your Obsidian folders and notes, using a layout inspired by NextJS routing. It helps you turn your Obsidian vault into a navigable dashboard, organizing content through a flexible folder system.

- **How It Works**: The dashboard automatically organizes files into rows and columns based on your folder structure, making it easy to visualize and access your notes directly from the Obsidian homepage.

- **Setup**: Simply paste the provided CSS and JavaScript files into your Obsidian settings to get started.

### QueueCards

The `QueueCards` feature helps you stay organized by creating a queue of notes to work through. You can skip items if you're not ready to deal with them yet, and each time you do, the skip counter goes up. This count also influences the color of the queued items, so you can see at a glance which items you've been avoiding.

- **Requirements**: For this to work, your notes need to have a `to` field in the frontmatter or metadata. This field can either be a string or an array of strings.

- **Setup**: Copy and paste the provided JavaScript and CSS files into your vault, and configure Templater to use the script.

## Getting Started

To use the contents of this repo, clone it or download the files to your local machine:

``` bash
git clone https://github.com/ZSturman/The-2nd-Best-Obsidian-Collection
```

Navigate to the specific feature directory you're interested in, and follow the instructions provided in the subfolder's README.

### Contribution
Feel free to fork this repo, add your features, or make improvements and send a pull request.

### Feedback and Suggestions
Got ideas or feedback? Open an issue in this repository, and let's make this truly the second best Obsidian collection out there!

# React + TypeScript + Vite

This template provides a minimal setup to get React working in Vite with HMR and some ESLint rules.

Currently, two official plugins are available:

- [@vitejs/plugin-react](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react/README.md) uses [Babel](https://babeljs.io/) for Fast Refresh
- [@vitejs/plugin-react-swc](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react-swc) uses [SWC](https://swc.rs/) for Fast Refresh

## Expanding the ESLint configuration

If you are developing a production application, we recommend updating the configuration to enable type aware lint rules:

- Configure the top-level `parserOptions` property like this:

```js
export default {
  // other rules...
  parserOptions: {
    ecmaVersion: 'latest',
    sourceType: 'module',
    project: ['./tsconfig.json', './tsconfig.node.json'],
    tsconfigRootDir: __dirname,
  },
}
```

- Replace `plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended` to `plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked` or `plugin:@typescript-eslint/strict-type-checked`
- Optionally add `plugin:@typescript-eslint/stylistic-type-checked`
- Install [eslint-plugin-react](https://github.com/jsx-eslint/eslint-plugin-react) and add `plugin:react/recommended` & `plugin:react/jsx-runtime` to the `extends` list

summary: Mind Adrift is a short-form narrative work centered on cognitive decline and disorientation, told from the inside rather than observed from a distance. It follows a person who is still present in the world—surrounded by family, routines, and familiar spaces—but increasingly unable to connect those pieces into a stable sense of reality. Time slips, relationships blur, and moments repeat or contradict one another without warning.The project is less about plot progression and more about structure and perspective. Scenes loop, reset, or fracture in ways that mirror how memory fails in real life. The goal is not to explain the condition or resolve it, but to place the audience inside that experience long enough to understand its logic and weight. It is designed for readers interested in character-driven narratives, psychological realism, and stories that prioritize internal experience over external action.
oneLiner: Mind Adrift is a narrative project about losing continuity with your own life while still moving through it, exploring memory, identity, and the quiet confusion of trying to stay oriented when your mind no longer cooperates.

The app handles both prescription and non-prescription meds, tracks strength and dosage, and keeps a full history of what you’ve taken. You can customize what each widget shows, set up alerts for refills, and rely on automatic calculations to keep your supply accurate without any extra effort.

readme.content:
# MediLedger

A **widget-first** iOS medication tracking app built with SwiftUI and WidgetKit. MediLedger helps you manage prescriptions and supplements with powerful home screen widgets for quick medication logging and at-a-glance tracking.

## Features

### 🎯 Widget-First Design
- **Interactive Home Screen Widgets**: Take medications, log half-doses, and refill prescriptions directly from your home screen
- **Customizable Widget Displays**: Choose from 15+ different data displays (pills remaining, daily average, streak tracking, etc.)
- **Real-Time Updates**: Widgets automatically update to reflect your latest medication intake

### 💊 Medication Management
- **Dual Medication Types**:
  - **Prescription**: Full refill tracking, days supply calculation, pharmacy information
  - **Non-Prescription**: Supplements, OTC medications, serving sizes, expiration dates
- **Flexible Dosing**: Log full doses, half doses, or custom amounts
- **Smart Tracking**: Automatic pill count updates, mg/dose tracking, and supply calculations

### 📊 Goal Tracking & Adherence
- **Intake Goals**: Set daily, weekly, or monthly adherence goals
- **Streak Tracking**: Monitor consecutive days of meeting your goals
- **Progress Monitoring**: Real-time progress bars and completion tracking
- **Specific Day Targeting**: Set goals for specific days of the week

### 📈 Analytics & Insights
- **7-Day Averages**: Track daily intake patterns over the past week
- **Refill Analytics**: Monitor average daily usage since last refill
- **Weekly Totals**: See cumulative intake for the current week
- **Historical Logs**: Complete medication history with timestamps

### 📤 Data Export
- **CSV Export**: Export medication logs for medical records or analysis
- **JSON Export**: Machine-readable format for data portability
- **Per-Medication or All**: Export individual medication logs or complete history

## Technical Stack

- **SwiftUI** & **SwiftData**: Modern Apple frameworks for UI and data persistence
- **WidgetKit**: Interactive home screen widgets with App Intents
- **AppIntents**: Deep integration for Siri shortcuts and widget actions
- **Swift 5.0**: Type-safe, modern Swift codebase

## Architecture

### Core Models
- `Med`: SwiftData model for medications with prescription/non-prescription support
- `MedLog`: Timestamped intake records with dosage tracking
- `MedEntity`: App Entity for widget integration and Siri support
- `IntakeGoal`: Goal tracking with period-based adherence

### Widget System
- `MedicationWidgetView`: Modular widget displays with 15+ configurable views
- `AppIntent`: Interactive widget actions (Take, TakeHalf, Refill)
- `MedTrackerWidget`: Widget timeline provider with real-time medication data

### Views
- `ContentView`: Master-detail medication list
- `MedicationDetailView`: Comprehensive medication information and actions
- `NewMedicationSheet`: Medication creation with progressive disclosure
- `MedicationGoalConfigView`: Goal setup and tracking

## Widget Display Options

The app supports **15+ customizable widget displays**:

### Universal Displays
- Pills Per Day Left
- Last Intake Time
- Total Pills Left
- Today's Total Intake
- 7-Day Average
- Pills Remaining
- This Week's Intake

### Goal-Based Displays
- Goal Progress (completed/target)
- Adherence Streak (consecutive days)
- Doses Left Today

### Prescription-Specific
- Average Since Refill
- Days Until Refill
- Refills Remaining

### Non-Prescription Specific
- Bottle Quantity Remaining

## Getting Started

### Requirements
- iOS 18.4+
- Xcode 16.0+
- Swift 5.0+

### Installation
1. Clone the repository:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/ZSturman/MediLedger.git
   ```

2. Open `MedTracker.xcodeproj` in Xcode

3. Build and run on your device or simulator

### Adding Your First Medication

1. Tap **"Add Medication"** button
2. Enter medication name and details
3. Choose **Prescription** or **Non-Prescription** type
4. Configure supply and dosage information
5. (Optional) Set an intake goal for adherence tracking
6. Tap **"Save"**

### Configuring Widgets

1. Long-press on your home screen
2. Tap the **+** button
3. Search for **"MediLedger"**
4. Add the small widget
5. Tap the widget to configure:
   - Select a medication
   - Choose primary display metric
   - Choose secondary display metric
6. Tap outside to save

### Taking Medications via Widget

- **Full Dose**: Tap the pill icon
- **Half Dose**: Tap the half-pill icon
- **Refill**: Use the refill action in the widget

## Project Structure

```
MedTracker/
├── MedTracker/                 # Main app target
│   ├── Models/                 # SwiftData models
│   │   ├── Medication.swift    # Core medication model
│   │   ├── MedEntity.swift     # App Entity for widgets
│   │   └── MedicationActions.swift # Medication business logic
│   ├── Views/                  # SwiftUI views
│   │   ├── MedicationDetailView.swift
│   │   ├── MedicationGoalConfigView.swift
│   │   └── MedicationActionButtons.swift
│   ├── Support/                # Helpers and utilities
│   │   ├── Enums.swift         # App enumerations
│   │   └── MedicationLogExporter.swift
│   ├── ContentView.swift       # Main list view
│   ├── NewMedicationSheet.swift # Add medication form
│   ├── Intents.swift           # App Intent implementations
│   └── SharedModel.swift       # Shared ModelContainer
│
├── MedTrackerWidget/           # Widget extension
│   ├── MedTrackerWidget.swift  # Widget definition
│   ├── MedicationWidgetView.swift # Widget UI components
│   ├── AppIntent.swift         # Widget configuration
│   └── MedTrackerWidgetBundle.swift
│
├── MedTrackerTests/            # Unit tests
└── MedTrackerUITests/          # UI tests
```

## Testing

The app includes comprehensive test coverage:

- **Unit Tests**: Medication logic, goal tracking, calculations
- **UI Tests**: Navigation, form validation, user interactions
- **Launch Tests**: Screenshots, orientation, accessibility

Run tests in Xcode: `⌘U`

## Data Persistence

- **SwiftData**: All medication data is stored locally using SwiftData
- **Shared Container**: Widget extension shares data with main app via shared model container
- **Privacy-First**: No cloud sync, all data stays on device

## Future Enhancements

- [ ] Scheduled dose reminders
- [ ] Multiple daily dose tracking
- [ ] Medication interaction warnings
- [ ] PDF prescription export
- [ ] Apple Watch companion app
- [ ] Medium/Large widget sizes

## Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Please feel free to submit a Pull Request.

## License

This project is available under the MIT License.

## Acknowledgments

Built with SwiftUI, SwiftData, and WidgetKit on iOS 18.4+

---

**Note**: This is a personal health tracking tool. Always consult with healthcare professionals for medical advice.

title: Drifting Priorities
name: Drifting Priorities
summary:
Drifting Priorities looks like a task app, but it is really a personal experiment in choosing work more honestly. It ranks tasks by combining time-based priority curves, recent avoidance, category recency, current state of mind, and dependency cost, then exposes that through both a CLI and a local web GUI. It also accepts small normalized telemetry imports and exports clean snapshots, which makes it feel more like a portable local system than a closed productivity app.

The audience seems fairly specific: people working alone or in a self-directed way, especially developers, writers, researchers, and other knowledge workers who do not trust a static list to tell the truth all day. What feels distinct here is the model, not the surface. The repo stays careful about explicit inputs, portability, and shared logic across interfaces. The part that still feels open is how tuned the scoring is in repeated real use, so I would describe it as thoughtful and usable, but still clearly in progress.
oneLiner: A local task recommender for days that do not stay flat, where what makes sense next can shift with time, energy, recent work, and complexity.

readme.content:
# Drifting Priorities ![Drifting Priorities hero](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/main/docs/assets/github-hero.svg) ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.11%2B-0f4c81?style=flat-square) ![Interface](https://img.shields.io/badge/interface-CLI%20%2B%20GUI-136f63?style=flat-square) ![Telemetry](https://img.shields.io/badge/telemetry-CSV%20%2F%20JSON-cf5c36?style=flat-square) ![Exports](https://img.shields.io/badge/exports-JSON%20%2B%20CSV%20ZIP-6a4c93?style=flat-square) Drifting Priorities is a context-aware task recommender for work that refuses to stay equally urgent all day. It combines task metadata, time-sensitive priority curves, recent activity, state-of-mind signals, and dependency penalties into a ranked "what should I do next?" view that you can drive from the command line or a local web GUI. ## Why It Exists Most task lists flatten everything into one static queue. Drifting Priorities is designed for situations where: - urgency changes over time, - repeated avoidance should matter, - your current energy or focus should influence the recommendation, - telemetry or external activity signals should feed the system, - data needs to stay portable instead of trapped in one interface. ## What You Get - A shared application layer used by both the CLI and the GUI. - A local Streamlit GUI with tabs for dashboard, tasks, state, visualization, telemetry import, and export. - Normalized telemetry import from CSV or JSON into activity and state signals. - Portable exports as a JSON snapshot or a CSV bundle ZIP. - Documentation and examples for automation, adapters, and external integrations. ## Interface Preview ![GUI preview](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/main/docs/assets/gui-preview.svg) The GUI mirrors the CLI rather than wrapping shell commands. Every task action runs through the same service layer used by `drift`. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR CLI["CLI (`drift`)"] --> Service["Shared application service"] GUI["GUI (`drift gui` / `drift-gui`)"] --> Service Telemetry["Telemetry adapters
CSV / JSON"] --> Service Service --> Engine["Priority engine"] Service --> Repo["SQLite repositories"] Engine --> Repo Repo --> DB[("SQLite database")] Service --> Export["Export builders
JSON / CSV ZIP"] ``` ## Quick Start ### 1. Install ```bash python3 -m pip install -e ".[gui,dev]" ``` If you only want the CLI and core library, install without extras: ```bash python3 -m pip install -e . ``` ### 2. Create Your First Task ```bash drift add "Write landing page copy" -c communication --days 2 --duration 45 drift add "Refine recommendation curve" -c research --days 5 --duration 90 drift next ``` ### 3. Launch the GUI ```bash drift gui ``` You can also use the dedicated launcher: ```bash drift-gui ``` ### 4. Set a Custom Database Location Use either a flag or an environment variable: ```bash drift --db-path ./workspace.db tasks DRIFT_DB_PATH=./workspace.db drift next ``` ## CLI Surface | Command | Purpose | GUI Equivalent | | --- | --- | --- | | `drift add` | Create a task with timing and curve settings | `Tasks -> Add Task` | | `drift next` | Show ranked recommendations | `Dashboard -> Recommended Next` | | `drift done` | Mark a task complete | `Dashboard` or `Tasks` action buttons | | `drift skip` | Skip a task and increase penance | `Dashboard` or `Tasks` action buttons | | `drift log` | Record category activity | `State & Activity -> Log Activity` | | `drift state` | View or update state-of-mind values | `State & Activity -> State of Mind` | | `drift tasks` | List active tasks with current scores | `Tasks -> Active Tasks` | | `drift viz` | Plot task priority curves | `Visualization` | | `drift remove` | Deactivate a task | `Tasks -> Remove` | | `drift import-telemetry` | Validate or import normalized telemetry | `Telemetry` | | `drift export` | Generate JSON or CSV bundle exports | `Export` | | `drift gui` | Launch the local web GUI | N/A | ## Telemetry Import Telemetry v1 uses a normalized schema so integrations can be added without rewriting the core engine. ### Supported record types - `activity`: written into `activity_log` - `state`: written into `user_state` and `user_state_history` ### CSV example ```csv kind,timestamp,category,task_id,description,key,value activity,2026-03-27T09:00:00,creative,,Morning sketch,, state,2026-03-27T09:15:00,,,,focus,0.90 ``` ### JSON example ```json { "records": [ { "kind": "activity", "timestamp": "2026-03-27T09:00:00", "category": "creative", "description": "Morning sketch" }, { "kind": "state", "timestamp": "2026-03-27T09:15:00", "key": "focus", "value": 0.9 } ] } ``` ### Import commands ```bash drift import-telemetry --format csv --input examples/telemetry/sample_telemetry.csv --preview drift import-telemetry --format json --input examples/telemetry/sample_telemetry.json ``` Validation rules are documented in [docs/telemetry.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/telemetry.md). ## Export Data Two export formats are available: - JSON snapshot: one portable file containing metadata, tasks, activity, completions, current state, and state history. - CSV bundle: a ZIP archive with table-level CSV files plus `metadata.json`. ```bash drift export --format json --output exports/drift-export.json drift export --format csv --output exports/drift-export.zip ``` The GUI exposes these as direct downloads in the `Export` tab. ## Automations and Integrations The app does not include a built-in scheduler. Instead, it exposes clean CLI and service boundaries so you can automate it with the tooling you already trust: - cron / launchd / Task Scheduler for recurring imports or exports, - wrapper scripts for wearable, note, or project-tool adapters, - local dashboards or notebooks that consume JSON / CSV exports, - future custom adapters that implement the normalized telemetry contract. Start with [docs/automations-integrations.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/automations-integrations.md) for working examples. ## Project Layout ```text src/ app.py shared service layer cli.py command line interface gui.py Streamlit launcher gui_app.py Streamlit UI telemetry.py CSV / JSON parsing and validation exporters.py JSON snapshot and CSV bundle exports docs/ gui.md telemetry.md exports.md automations-integrations.md examples/ telemetry/ tests/ ``` ## Docs Index - [docs/README.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/README.md) - [docs/gui.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/gui.md) - [docs/telemetry.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/telemetry.md) - [docs/exports.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/exports.md) - [docs/automations-integrations.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Drifting-Priorities/blob/main/docs/automations-integrations.md) ## Development Run the test suite: ```bash python3 -m pytest -q ``` Run the CLI directly from source: ```bash python3 -m src.cli tasks ``` Launch the GUI from source: ```bash python3 -m src.gui ```

– Short study or review sessions.
– Keeping a meaningful note visible through the day.


Top Note makes productivity manageable by narrowing your focus. Instead of managing a list, you engage with one small task—and let time handle the rest.

readme.content:
# Top Note

Top Note is a widget-first productivity app that keeps you focused on what’s next—literally one thing at a time. Instead of endless to‑do lists you never look at, Top Note presents a single reminder, flashcard, or note right on your Home Screen widget.

Download on the App Store:
https://apps.apple.com/us/app/top-note/id6742725271

## Why it works
Top Note introduces gentle friction: there’s no long list to cherry-pick from. You must either complete the next item or see it again soon, keeping important tasks and knowledge top-of-mind without overwhelming you.

## Core features
- One-at-a-time reminders — No scrolling, no distraction.  
- Skip & repeat — Skipped items return sooner so you can’t forget them.  
- Spaced repetition flashcards — Boost memory and recall with confidence-rated reviews.  
- Widget-first design — Most interactions happen directly from the Home Screen.  
- Flexible notes — Keep quotes, mantras, micro-goals, or short reminders visible all day.  
- Simple setup — Configure everything in the main app; use it primarily from the widget.

## Perfect for
- People who ignore traditional reminders.  
- Habit building and routine reinforcement.  
- Quick study sessions with spaced repetition.  
- Keeping a short note or mantra in plain sight.

## Quick start
1. Install Top Note from the App Store.  
2. Open the app and create a note, reminder, or flashcard.  
3. Add the Top Note widget to your Home Screen.  
4. Use the widget to complete, skip, or review the single item it shows.

## Privacy
Top Note is designed for simplicity; local-first storage is preferred where possible. Consult the app’s privacy settings for details on syncing and backups.

## Feedback & support
For bugs, feature requests, or support, use the contact option inside the app or submit feedback through the App Store page.

When an experiment goes wrong, fragments of the device scatter across different eras—from the Hadean eon to the modern age—opening unstable portals that pull ancient life and historical figures into the present. The game blends exploration, puzzle-solving, and light combat as players travel through time, retrieve lost machine parts, and face the consequences of a fractured timeline. Designed for players who enjoy narrative exploration and gradual skill-building, Broken Time Machine connects history, science, and discovery into a single continuous journey of repair and adaptation.
oneLiner: A Unity-based adventure game where a scientist’s assistant repairs a broken time machine by traveling through Earth’s history, collecting its missing parts, and restoring order to a fractured timeline.

# Broken Time Machine

Broken Time Machine is now organized as a two-track repo:

- `BTM_3D_Browser/`: the active Babylon.js + TypeScript browser game.
- `V1_SideScroller/`: the preserved Python/Pygame side-scrolling prototype.
- `docs/`: architecture, gameplay, schema, and migration notes.
- `_original_design/`: archived concept writing and legacy Unity reference material.

## Active Build: `BTM_3D_Browser`

This is the current vertical-slice target: a third-person 3D browser adventure where an underqualified lab assistant fixes history one prehistoric retrieval job at a time.

### Features in the current 3D slice

- main menu and browser boot flow
- lab intro tutorial
- evolving present-day hub with staged corruption
- Archean retrieval mission with lava escape sequence
- hub creature capture loop with research-point upgrades
- Cambrian dive mission with oxygen pressure and oversized creature aggro
- HTML/CSS overlays for HUD, dialogue, field log, pause, and upgrade station
- local save persistence with manifest validation and gameplay logic tests

### Run locally

```bash
cd BTM_3D_Browser
npm install
npm run dev
```

### Build and test

```bash
cd BTM_3D_Browser
npm run test
npm run build
```

## Legacy Build: `V1_SideScroller`

This is the frozen 2D Python prototype preserved for reference and comparison. It is still runnable as its own self-contained prototype.

```bash
cd V1_SideScroller
pip install -r requirements.txt
python main.py
```

## Docs

- `docs/architecture.md`: runtime layout, scene flow, and major systems
- `docs/gameplay-loop.md`: story beats, progression rhythm, and player-facing flow
- `docs/content-schema.md`: data contracts for eras, creatures, missions, and saves
- `docs/migration-notes.md`: what changed from the side scroller to the browser version

## Project direction

The browser game is intentionally a polished vertical slice rather than a full content-complete campaign. The repo is now set up so more eras, hazards, upgrades, and hub corruption stages can be added without changing the core data contracts.

summary: An event-segmented episodic memory agent designed to simulate how an intelligent system perceives, interprets, and organizes experience over time. The architecture centers on a strict cognitive loop—resolving location, identifying entities, detecting change—and freezing those moments into structured episodes. Built with swappable modules, strict invariants, and full logging, the system prioritizes learning over hardcoded assumptions, user-defined semantics over predefined categories, and reproducible behavior over opaque heuristics.
oneLiner: A modular cognitive agent framework that models how experience becomes memory.

readme.content:
# Episodic Memory

This repository is now organized around one canonical Python package, `episodic_memory`, and one canonical web app in `dashboard/`.

The merged project is offline-first and built around a single artifact bundle that notebooks, the inspector API, and the Next.js dashboard all read.

## What It Does

- runs deterministic stub scenarios against the canonical working-memory core
- runs panorama image or video exploration for location identification
- records one canonical run bundle for every execution
- exposes read-only inspection through Jupyter notebooks and the dashboard
- imports legacy `episodic-memory-agent` run directories into the canonical bundle format

## Canonical Layout

- `src/episodic_memory/`: canonical Python package
- `dashboard/`: canonical Next.js dashboard
- `notebooks/`: canonical notebook workspace
- `docs/`: architecture, runtime, validation, migration, and notebook docs
- `archive/`: archived legacy codebases after migration

## Install

```bash
pip install -e .[dev,notebook]
```

## Run

```bash
# Stub scenario
episodic-memory run --mode stub --scenario scenario_e

# Panorama location-identification flow
episodic-memory run --mode panorama --image-dir tests/fixtures/panorama

# Import an old episodic-memory-agent run
episodic-memory import-legacy archive/episodic-memory-agent/runs/<run_dir>

# Start the read-only inspector API
episodic-memory inspect --run-root runs
```

## Dashboard

```bash
cd dashboard
npm install
npm run dev
```

The dashboard expects the inspector API at `http://127.0.0.1:8780` by default. Override with `INSPECTOR_API_BASE` if needed.

## Notebook Workspace

```bash
jupyter lab
```

Canonical notebooks:

- `notebooks/01_pipeline_workspace.ipynb`
- `notebooks/02_memory_explorer.ipynb`
- `notebooks/03_run_comparison_and_dashboard_parity.ipynb`

## Canonical Artifact Bundle

Every run writes:

- `manifest.json`
- `summary.json`
- `memories.json`
- `graph.json`
- `events.jsonl`
- `memory_decisions.jsonl`
- `steps/step_XXXX.snapshot.json`

This is the shared contract used by notebooks, the inspector API, and the dashboard.

## Unity Status

Unity remains part of the long-term architecture but is intentionally deferred in this merged v1. The current canonical runtime supports `stub` and `panorama` modes end-to-end.

## Docs

- [Docs Index](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/README.md)
- [Architecture](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/architecture.md)
- [Runtime Flow](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/runtime-flow.md)
- [Notebook Workspace](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/notebooks.md)
- [Memory Transparency](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/memory-transparency.md)
- [Validation Guide](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/validation.md)
- [Status](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/status.md)
- [Migration Map](https://github.com/ZSturman/Episodic-Memory-Agent/blob/main/docs/migration-map.md)

title: My Notion Pipeline
name: My Notion Pipeline
subtitle: Notion-powered project operations and portfolio automation
summary:
An automated project and portfolio workflow that uses Notion as the central source of truth for capturing, organizing, maintaining, and publishing work across its full lifecycle. What began as a folder-based system built on local files and .project.json evolved into a relational Notion workspace that connects project metadata, work logs, tasks, milestones, media, and documentation in one structured environment.


Inside Notion, the system uses formulas, required-action logic, severity scoring, and visual workflow signals to make project readiness measurable and easier to manage. That structure reduces duplicate entry, improves documentation quality, and keeps projects consistently maintained as they move from idea to execution to presentation.


When project data reaches the right state, n8n detects qualifying changes and assembles portfolio-ready JSON, which is then sent into a Python-based build pipeline that prepares content and media for a static portfolio site. Linear supports issue tracking, GitHub stores code and optimized assets, and Firebase Hosting serves the finished site.


The result is a connected operating system for project operations and portfolio publishing: one that keeps documentation, execution, and presentation aligned with the real state of the work over time.
oneLiner: A Notion-centered project operating system that turns structured project data into an always-up-to-date portfolio.
